text.title: Flexibility and Efficiency — Core Rules
text.summary: Concrete rules for implementing Nielsen Heuristic #7: layered accelerators, multiple paths, progressive disclosure, customization, and bulk operations.

rule.statement: Provide keyboard shortcuts for all frequently repeated actions; display them in menus, tooltips, and a discoverable shortcut panel.
rule.rationale: Shortcuts without discoverability give zero efficiency gain. Layered exposure (menu label → tooltip → panel) ensures novices learn them gradually while experts use them immediately.

rule.statement: Follow platform conventions before inventing custom shortcuts: Ctrl+S = Save, Ctrl+Z = Undo, Ctrl+C/V = Copy/Paste, Ctrl+F = Find.
rule.rationale: Custom shortcuts that conflict with platform standards cause errors and erode trust. Conventions are non-negotiable.

rule.statement: Test all custom shortcuts against NVDA+Chrome and VoiceOver+Safari before shipping — screen readers capture many key combinations.
rule.rationale: Accessibility tools intercept keyboard events at the OS level; untested shortcuts silently break screen reader workflows.

rule.statement: Provide at least two paths to accomplish each primary task (e.g., menu click, double-click, right-click context menu, keyboard shortcut).
rule.rationale: Different users have different motor skills, device types, and learned habits. A single path is an accessibility and efficiency bottleneck.

rule.statement: Ensure that all paths to the same goal produce identical outcomes — diverging behavior between paths fragments user mental models.
rule.rationale: If double-click edits inline but right-click opens a modal, users cannot build a reliable mental model of how editing works.

rule.statement: Show essential options by default; hide advanced options behind a clearly labeled toggle or "Advanced" section.
rule.rationale: Exposing all options simultaneously overwhelms novices without benefiting experts, who know where to find advanced controls.

rule.statement: Show contextual options based on the current selection — hide unrelated options, do not grey them out in long lists.
rule.rationale: Greyed-out irrelevant options create visual noise and force users to filter mentally what should be filtered by the system.

rule.statement: For any action that can be performed on a single item, provide a bulk version that operates on a user-defined selection.
rule.rationale: Power users managing 50+ items cannot afford per-item actions; bulk ops are the primary efficiency multiplier for high-volume workflows.

rule.statement: Always show a count confirmation before executing destructive bulk operations: "Delete 14 items?"
rule.rationale: Irreversible bulk actions without confirmation introduce data loss risk that no shortcut convenience justifies.

rule.statement: Provide customization (saved layouts, pinned items, custom shortcuts) only after default shortcuts and workflows are proven insufficient.
rule.rationale: Premature customization multiplies QA surface and creates its own cognitive load for novice users who encounter a non-default state.

rule.statement: Always expose a "Reset to defaults" option alongside any customization to prevent users from getting permanently lost.
rule.rationale: Users who customize and then forget their changes have no recovery path without a reset option.

text.reference: Audit template for shortcuts, customization, alternative paths, and power-user features
Script to extract keyboard shortcut bindings from a JS/TS codebase
